content:
Most investors spend their careers looking for things everyone else has missed. Usually that means technologies, founders or markets. Occasionally, though, the opportunity isn’t a product at all. It’s a blind spot.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I found myself thinking about this while reading a recent article in The Economist. It compared the values embedded within 25 of the world’s leading AI models using data from the World Values Survey, a project that has spent more than forty years mapping how societies think about family, religion, authority, politics and everyday life.
The conclusions weren’t especially surprising. Models built in the West broadly reflected Western assumptions. Chinese models reflected the environment in which they were developed. That was the headline, and understandably so.
What interested me was something almost mentioned in passing.
None of the models closely represented the prevailing worldviews found across much of Africa or the Muslim world.
It’s an easy sentence to read and move on from. But I don’t think we should.
Technology has always carried traces of the societies that created it. Steam engines reflected industrial Britain. Silicon Valley shaped the internet. American payment systems changed the way much of the world buys goods. We often mistake technologies for being universal when they’re actually local ideas that proved exportable.
Artificial intelligence feels different because it doesn’t simply help us do things. Increasingly, it helps us think.
Ask someone why they use ChatGPT or Claude every day and the answer is rarely, “to find information”. Search engines already solved that problem. People use AI to untangle emails, think through difficult conversations, weigh competing options and pressure-test decisions. They’re outsourcing parts of the thinking process itself.
That’s a big shift.
It also means we should care much more about the assumptions these systems quietly inherit.
There’s a tendency to frame this as another debate about bias, as though the challenge is simply removing politics from artificial intelligence. I’m not convinced that’s possible, or even desirable. Every society makes different trade-offs. Every legal system reflects different priorities. Every culture develops its own understanding of responsibility, fairness and authority. Pretending those differences don’t exist doesn’t make them disappear.
The more interesting question is what happens when billions of people increasingly rely on systems that weren’t built with their own experiences in mind.
Markets have encountered versions of this problem before.
For years, multinational companies believed they could enter emerging economies with products designed elsewhere, adding a local language and perhaps a local advertising campaign along the way. Sometimes that worked. More often, local competitors eventually won because they understood something deeper than translation. They understood behaviour.
The companies that dominate India’s digital payments market aren’t simply the ones with the best software. They’re the ones that understand how people actually move money. The same is true of retail, healthcare and media almost everywhere in the world. Culture isn’t a finishing touch applied after the engineering is complete. It’s part of the product.
Artificial intelligence may prove to be no different.
Much of today’s conversation revolves around compute, model size and reasoning capability. Those are important measures, but they’re also the easiest things to compare. Investors naturally gravitate towards metrics they can quantify.
The harder question is whether intelligence eventually becomes abundant while cultural understanding remains scarce.
If that happens, today’s competitive landscape starts to look very different.
Building the smartest model in the world won’t necessarily create the most valuable business if another company builds the model that people trust most. Trust has always been contextual. We trust doctors because they understand our circumstances. We trust advisers because they understand our incentives. Increasingly, we’ll trust AI for exactly the same reason.
That’s one reason I’m watching regional AI initiatives with growing interest.
Across the Gulf, governments aren’t investing only because artificial intelligence promises economic growth. They’re investing because they understand something else. Language, culture and institutional knowledge are forms of infrastructure. For decades we thought about infrastructure as ports, airports, roads and fibre optic cables. The next generation will also include models capable of understanding the societies they serve.
That doesn’t mean every country should build its own isolated intelligence.
Quite the opposite.
One of the greatest strengths of AI is its ability to connect knowledge across borders. Losing that would be an extraordinary mistake. But there’s an equally obvious danger in assuming one relatively narrow set of cultural assumptions can adequately represent eight billion people.
The challenge isn’t choosing between global and local.
It’s learning how to build both at once.
I’ve found myself returning to that tension repeatedly over the past few years, not only when thinking about artificial intelligence but also biotechnology, sport, media and investment. It’s one reason I began writing under the banner of The Human Code.
The Human Code isn’t a prediction about where technology is heading. It’s a way of interrogating technologies before they become invisible parts of everyday life. Whenever I encounter a major innovation, I ask the same questions.

• Who benefits?

• What endures?

• What quietly disappears?
Those questions don’t produce neat answers. They’re intended to slow us down long enough to notice what excitement often obscures.
Artificial intelligence passes many of those tests brilliantly. It will make expertise more accessible, lower barriers to innovation and almost certainly create extraordinary economic value.
The unanswered question is whether it will also become more representative.
Reading The Economist, I wasn’t struck by the differences between Western and Chinese models. Those differences were expected — we already know.
I was struck by the absence of everyone who wasn’t really present in either.
For investors, that clearly looks like an underserved market.
For policymakers, it looks like a strategic challenge.
For technologists, it looks like a design problem.
Viewed through the lens of The Human Code, it looks like something larger still.
The systems we build eventually shape the societies that build them next. If artificial intelligence becomes part of humanity’s cognitive infrastructure, then ensuring more of humanity can genuinely recognise itself within that infrastructure isn’t simply an ethical ambition. It may turn out to be one of the defining commercial opportunities of the AI era.
The companies that understand that earliest may not build the biggest models.
They may build the ones the world feels were built for it.

About Nicole Junkermann
Nicole Junkermann is an international investor focused on technology, sports and media. She leads NJF Holdings, a global investment group, and its sports platform Gameday by NJF Holdings, which invests in sports leagues, media rights and technology-driven fan engagement. Her work in the sector focuses on building long-term sports infrastructure and expanding the commercial and global reach of professional leagues.
